items.question_text: Unless otherwise agreed, what contact should be maintained with a NAVAID flight-inspection aircraft?

choices.text: Direct contact with the pilot, along with exchange of known traffic information and intentions.
Relay-only coordination through another controller so the inspection crew can limit radio use.
No direct contact once the aircraft starts an automatic inspection run.
Contact only when another aircraft enters the same ARTCC sector.

items.explanation: Per 9-1-1, flight-inspection aircraft receive maximum assistance and, unless otherwise agreed, direct contact is maintained with the pilot while known traffic information and intentions are exchanged.

items.question_text: What does a Flight Check aircraft's statement "recorded run" mean operationally?

choices.text: The inspection is in progress and requires the full runway environment and the entire procedure, including the missed approach if needed, without interruption.
The pilot is available for vectors off the procedure as long as the aircraft remains within 5 miles of course.
Only the final approach segment matters, so the missed approach and runway protection are optional.
The call only indicates that the aircraft is recording ATC audio for later review.

items.explanation: Per 9-1-2, a reported "recorded run" means the automated inspection is in progress, the entire procedure may have to be flown including the missed approach, and any interruption requires the run to be reinitiated.

items.question_text: What altitude range may a preplanned automatic flight check require the pilot to fly?

choices.text: From 1,000 feet above the antenna site up to the minimum en route altitude.
From the airport traffic pattern altitude up to FL180 only.
Only at or above the minimum en route altitude for the entire mission.
From the runway surface up to 500 feet above the antenna site.

items.explanation: Per 9-1-3, preplanned automatic flight places altitude limitations on the pilot that can range from 1,000 feet above the antenna site up to the MEA.

items.question_text: During an en route descent of a military aircraft carrying dangerous materials, what routing restriction applies unless the pilot concurs?

choices.text: Do not vector the aircraft from its planned route.
Do not assign any altitude change until the aircraft reaches the destination airport.
Do not provide traffic information to the pilot.
Do not allow the aircraft to continue on its filed route if dangerous cargo is on board.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-1, when an aircraft carrying dangerous materials is provided an en route descent, it is not vectored from the planned route unless the pilot concurs.

items.question_text: Within the conterminous United States, when may celestial navigation training be approved?

choices.text: Only for transponder-equipped aircraft within ARTCC radar coverage.
For any military aircraft as long as the pilot stays within 30 NM of course.
Only outside ARTCC radar coverage so conventional navigation can be fully suspended.
Only after control transfer to the next facility has already been completed.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-2, celestial navigation training in the conterminous United States is limited to transponder-equipped aircraft operating within ARTCC radar coverage.

items.question_text: Once special handling for an experimental aircraft has been approved, when may the pilot be asked to deviate from the planned action?

choices.text: Only to preclude an emergency situation.
Whenever a more efficient routing becomes available.
Any time another controller requests a change for sequencing convenience.
Only after the aircraft enters Class B airspace.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-3, once special handling for an experimental aircraft is approved, the pilot is not asked to deviate from the planned action except to preclude an emergency situation.

items.question_text: What approval is required before special flight procedures may be used for FAA research and development test activities?

choices.text: Approval by the facility air traffic manager.
Approval by the pilot in command alone once traffic permits.
Approval by the receiving ARTCC after the flight departs.
Approval by airport operations only if the flight remains below FL180.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-4, special flight procedures for FAA research and development test activities must be coordinated in advance, traffic must permit, and the facility air traffic manager must approve the procedures before use.

items.question_text: What must the first ATC facility establishing contact with a FLYNET aircraft do besides provide expeditious handling?

choices.text: Report it to the OS or CIC for DEN notification and add to the flight plan remarks that DEN notification has been accomplished.
Issue only normal priority handling unless the pilot separately declares an emergency.
Delay any security notification until the aircraft has been handed off to the destination facility.
Cancel the flight plan and create a new one marked as a law-enforcement mission.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-5, the first facility establishing contact with a FLYNET aircraft provides expeditious handling, reports it to the OS/CIC for DEN reporting, and adds to the flight plan remarks that DEN notification has been accomplished.

items.question_text: On a designated MARSA military training route, who assumes separation responsibility after the aircraft passes the primary or alternate entry fix until ATC reestablishes separation after route operations are completed?

choices.text: The military.
The departure ARTCC.
The receiving ARTCC only after the aircraft exits the route.
The tanker aircraft if one is on frequency.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-6, for designated MARSA routes the military assumes separation responsibility after the aircraft passes the primary or alternate entry fix until ATC establishes separation after MTR operations are completed.

items.question_text: What must ATC do if the requesting ADCF asks for transfer of control jurisdiction of interceptors on an active air defense mission?

choices.text: Expedite the transfer of control jurisdiction upon request.
Delay the transfer until routine sector workload decreases.
Refuse transfer until the unknown aircraft has been intercepted visually.
Transfer jurisdiction only after the interceptors leave Class A airspace.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-7, ATC provides maximum assistance for active air defense scrambles and must expedite transfer of control jurisdiction to the requesting ADCF when asked.

items.question_text: What should be done with reports of unusual aircraft activity near a special interest site such as a refinery or nuclear plant?

choices.text: Immediately relay the report or information to supervisory or CIC personnel.
Hold the report unless a second aircraft confirms the activity.
Begin independent monitoring of the site even if supervisory personnel have not directed it.
Advise the reporting pilot that ATC does not accept such reports.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-8, any report or information about unusual aircraft activity near a special interest site is immediately relayed to supervisory or CIC personnel.

items.question_text: Which statement correctly describes Special Flight Rules Areas and special air traffic rules?

choices.text: Not every area listed in 14 CFR part 93 is an SFRA, but all SFRAs are subject to the special air traffic rules in part 93 unless otherwise authorized by ATC.
Every area listed in 14 CFR part 93 is automatically an SFRA.
Special air traffic rules apply only when a controller chooses to activate them for the day.
An area cannot be an SFRA unless it also contains Class B airspace.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-9, special air traffic rules are prescribed in 14 CFR part 93 for certain designated areas, not all of those areas are SFRAs, and all SFRAs are subject to those special rules unless ATC otherwise authorizes.

items.question_text: What is required for operations in the Washington, DC SFRA security service area?

choices.text: Two-way radio communications, flight planning, and an operational transponder on an assigned code.
Voice communications only, because a transponder is optional if the pilot can report position.
An assigned route only, because ATC security tracking replaces normal transponder requirements.
Mode C operation only, because a flight plan is not needed inside security services airspace.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-10, operations in the DC SFRA security services area require two-way radio communications, flight planning, and an operational transponder on an assigned code.

items.question_text: If information on the subject aircraft is not known when a SECNOT is received, what should the controller do?

choices.text: Broadcast the call sign on all frequencies and advise the OS or CIC of the response.
Wait for the next handoff before informing the OS or CIC.
Issue an immediate 7700 verification to any aircraft with a similar call sign.
Suspend frequency monitoring until another facility reports positive identification.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-11, all information on the subject aircraft is forwarded to the OS/CIC, and if the information is not known the controller broadcasts the call sign on all frequencies and advises the OS/CIC of the response.

items.question_text: If requested by the pilot or flight crew of a law enforcement aircraft, what communications accommodation should be provided whenever possible?

choices.text: Communicate on a separate and unique communications frequency.
Move the aircraft to 121.5 regardless of other traffic impacts.
Use company frequency only and stop all ATC communications.
Communicate exclusively through another law enforcement aircraft.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-12, law enforcement aircraft are given maximum assistance and, when requested by the pilot or crew, should be handled on a separate and unique communications frequency whenever possible.

items.clearance: CLEARED TO CONDUCT REFUELING ALONG 14 TRACK, MAINTAIN BLOCK FL210 THROUGH FL230.

choices.text: Cleared to conduct refueling along 14 track, maintain block FL210 through FL230.
Cleared to conduct refueling along 14 track, maintain FL210.
Cleared to conduct refueling from 14 track, descend to FL230.
Cleared to conduct refueling along 14 track, maintain block FL230 through FL210.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-13 phraseology, an aerial refueling clearance may be issued as 'CLEARED TO CONDUCT REFUELING ALONG (number) TRACK ... MAINTAIN BLOCK (altitude) THROUGH (altitude).'

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: COMMENCING AT FL270, DESCENDING TO FL250.
items.display_text: COMMENCING AT FL270 CLIMBING TO FL250
items.explanation: Per 9-2-13 phraseology, the format is 'COMMENCING AT (altitude), DESCENDING TO (altitude).'

items.situation: A receiver aircraft has departed the ARIP and is now 3 miles from the tanker during a refueling mission. Another controller wants to assign it a new beacon code immediately to simplify downstream coordination.
items.para_context: Do not request a receiver aircraft that has been cleared to conduct air refueling and has departed the ARIP to make code changes when it is less than 5 miles from the tanker. Once rendezvous is completed, the tanker must keep receiver aircraft within 3 miles until MARSA is terminated.

choices.text: Do not request the code change, because a receiver aircraft less than 5 miles from the tanker must not be asked to change codes.
Issue the code change now because 3 miles from the tanker is inside the normal refueling formation and therefore best for retagging.
Request the receiver to squawk standby instead, because code changes are prohibited only when the aircraft is inside 1 mile of the tanker.
Cancel MARSA first and then require the code change regardless of the receiver's distance from the tanker.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-13b1, do not request code changes from a receiver aircraft less than 5 miles from the tanker after it has departed the ARIP.

items.question_text: When does MARSA begin between a tanker and receiver aircraft during aerial refueling?

choices.text: When the tanker and receiver have entered the refueling airspace and the tanker advises ATC that it is accepting MARSA.
As soon as the receiver is within 3 miles of the tanker, even before the tanker accepts responsibility.
Only after the receiver has completed the rendezvous and resumed normal squawk.
When ATC assigns the block altitude, regardless of the tanker’s advisement.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-13, MARSA begins between the tanker and receiver when both have entered the air refueling airspace and the tanker advises ATC that it is accepting MARSA.

items.question_text: What is the primary method described for separating two supersonic aircraft above FL600?

choices.text: Descend the aircraft at the lower flight level and provide vertical separation.
Vector the higher aircraft through turns until 5 miles lateral separation exists.
Keep both aircraft level and use 1,000 feet until radar separation can be applied.
Assign the same flight level and stagger the entry fix times by 1 minute.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-14, the primary method described for separating two supersonic aircraft is to descend the aircraft at the lower flight level and provide vertical separation.

items.question_text: Which operation is specifically authorized to use a military special use frequency?

choices.text: All aircraft during supersonic flight.
Any civil aircraft flying at night in Class A airspace.
Any formation flight of two or more aircraft in day VMC.
Only tanker aircraft inside an aerial refueling block altitude.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-15, special use frequencies are assigned to certain military operations, including all aircraft during supersonic flight.

items.question_text: What should controllers do when a proposed flight path will traverse a reported or forecast hazardous-radiation area?

choices.text: Advise the pilot and reroute the aircraft if the pilot requests it.
Wait until the aircraft enters the area before discussing it with the pilot.
Reroute the aircraft automatically without telling the pilot why.
Do nothing unless another aircraft has already reported radiation in flight.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-16, controllers advise pilots whenever their proposed flight path will traverse a reported or forecast hazardous-radiation area and reroute the aircraft when requested.

items.question_text: How should inflight clearance requests from SAMP flights be handled?

choices.text: Approve altitude and route change requests to the maximum extent possible, even if other IFR aircraft have to be recleared.
Deny inflight changes so the sampling mission stays on its original route regardless of conditions.
Limit assistance to altitude changes only and never approve route changes.
Treat the flight like any other IFR aircraft and provide no special handling.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-17, SAMP flights receive special handling and their inflight altitude and route change requests are approved to the maximum extent possible, including reclearing other IFR aircraft if necessary.

items.question_text: Which control action is acceptable while expediting an AWACS or NORAD Special flight to its destination orbit?

choices.text: An en route altitude change within plus or minus 2,000 feet of the requested flight level.
A lengthy delay sequence followed by a major reroute that moves the flight away from the orbit.
Holding the aircraft until all routine arrival traffic has been worked first.
Any vector or route change, even if it impedes progress toward the destination orbit.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-18, AWACS/NORAD Special flights are not delayed, and acceptable actions while expediting them include en route altitude changes within plus or minus 2,000 feet and minor vectors or route changes that do not impede progress.

items.question_text: When advising a TEAL or NOAA mission aircraft about traffic near a dropsonde release, what information must the advisory include?

choices.text: The traffic's altitude, direction of flight, and ETA at the point closest to the drop area or the drop fix.
Only the traffic's call sign and whether it is IFR or VFR.
Only the exact weight and descent rate of the dropsonde.
Only the location of the next downstream sector boundary.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-19, when workload and priorities permit, traffic advisories for dropsonde releases include the traffic's altitude, direction of flight, and ETA at the closest point to the drop area or fix.

items.question_text: What must ATC specify, as necessary, when approving an evasive action maneuver?

choices.text: The route segment, the maximum route deviation from centerline in miles, and the altitude block for the maneuver.
Only the aircraft type and the name of the target site.
Only the block altitude, because route and deviation are left entirely to the pilot.
Only the complete maneuver time and the weather at the recovery base.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-20, an evasive action maneuver is approved only on the basis of a permissible traffic situation and ATC specifies, as necessary, the route segment, maximum deviation from centerline, and altitude.

items.question_text: What should controllers understand about nonstandard formation or cell operations?

choices.text: Controllers should be knowledgeable of the various tactics employed and the procedures used.
Controllers should treat them exactly like standard formations in every case and ignore tactical differences.
Controllers should automatically apply individual-aircraft separation to every member at all times.
Controllers should stop providing ATC services whenever a nonstandard cell is involved.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-21, controllers should be knowledgeable of the tactics employed in nonstandard cell operations and the procedures used for them.

items.question_text: Which condition makes these special-use airspace procedures inapplicable?

choices.text: The restricted area, warning area, MOA, ATCAA, or stationary ALTRV has been released to the controlling agency.
The aircraft is IFR and within 10 minutes of the boundary.
The using agency has not yet been called by the pilot directly.
The airspace is active and no LOA or LOP exists.

items.explanation: Per 9-3-1, these procedures do not apply when one of the listed exceptions exists, including when the airspace has been released to the controlling agency.

items.question_text: What vertical buffer is used to separate a nonparticipating aircraft from the upper or lower limit of active special use airspace, ATCAA, or a stationary ALTRV?

choices.text: At least 500 feet above or below the airspace limits, or 1,000 feet when above FL290.
Exactly 1,000 feet above or below at all altitudes.
At least 200 feet above or below unless a LOA specifies otherwise.
Only the standard semicircular altitude rule, with no buffer from the special use airspace limits.

items.explanation: Per 9-3-2, nonparticipating aircraft are separated by assigning an altitude at least 500 feet above or below the upper or lower limit of the active airspace, and 1,000 feet when above FL290.

items.question_text: If an aircraft operating VFR-on-top may enter an active prohibited, restricted, warning area, MOA, or ATCAA, what VFR-on-top altitude instruction should be given?

choices.text: Maintain VFR-on-top at least 500 feet above the upper limit or below the lower limit of the airspace across the affected segment.
Maintain VFR-on-top exactly at the midpoint of the block altitude used by the special use airspace.
Maintain VFR-on-top at least 5 miles laterally from the airspace with no altitude instruction needed.
Maintain VFR-on-top only if the pilot agrees to enter the airspace at the filed IFR altitude.

items.explanation: Per 9-3-3, when the route, track, or altitude may cause entry into active special use airspace or an ATCAA, the aircraft is instructed to maintain VFR-on-top at least 500 feet above the upper limit or below the lower limit of the airspace.

items.question_text: If an LOA or LOP exists and permission has been granted to transit active special use airspace or an ATCAA, what should ATC do?

choices.text: Comply with the instructions or clearances issued by the using agency and provide the applicable separation minima between transiting aircraft.
Ignore the using agency’s instructions and apply ordinary facility routes instead.
Automatically deny all transit because the FAA cannot authorize any operation there.
Issue the transit only if the pilot waives separation services in writing.

items.explanation: Per 9-3-4, when a LOA or LOP has been coordinated and permission is granted to transit the area, ATC complies with the using agency instructions or clearances and applies the required separation minima.

items.question_text: When told an aircraft plans to dump fuel, what information should be determined first?

choices.text: The route and altitude to be flown and the weather conditions in which the operation will be conducted.
Only the number of persons on board and the destination airport.
Only whether the aircraft is IFR or VFR, because the route and weather do not matter.
Only the estimated duration of the dump and the fuel remaining after it ends.

items.explanation: Per 9-4-1, when information is received that an aircraft plans to dump fuel, ATC determines the route and altitude to be flown and the weather conditions in which the operation will be conducted.

items.question_text: When may an aircraft dumping fuel be requested to fly a different route?

choices.text: When the dump is not for emergency reasons, even if the aircraft is in IFR conditions.
Never, because a fuel-dumping aircraft must remain on its original route.
Only when the aircraft is VFR; IFR aircraft may never be rerouted during fuel dumping.
Only after the dump is complete and the aircraft is ready for descent.

items.explanation: Per 9-4-2, except when the aircraft is dumping fuel for emergency reasons, it may be requested to fly a different route in either VFR or IFR conditions.

items.question_text: If an aircraft is dumping fuel in IFR conditions, what altitude assignment is required?

choices.text: At least 2,000 feet above the highest obstacle within 5 miles of the route or pattern being flown.
Exactly 1,000 feet above the MEA, regardless of nearby obstacles.
Any altitude that remains above the airport elevation by 500 feet.
At least 2,000 feet above the highest obstacle within 10 miles of the route or pattern.

items.explanation: Per 9-4-3, in IFR fuel-dumping operations the aircraft is assigned an altitude at least 2,000 feet above the highest obstacle within 5 miles of the route or pattern being flown.

items.situation: A radar-identified VFR aircraft is maneuvering near an aircraft dumping fuel. Another controller wants to treat the situation like ordinary VFR traffic advisories and use only 3 miles because the VFR aircraft is being watched on radar.
items.para_context: Known IFR aircraft are separated from the aircraft dumping fuel by 1,000 feet above or the greater Chapter 4 vertical minimum, 2,000 feet below, 5 miles radar, or 5 miles laterally. VFR radar-identified aircraft are separated by 5 miles and by the applicable radar procedures.

choices.text: Separate the radar-identified VFR aircraft by 5 miles and apply the applicable radar procedures.
Use only 3 miles because any radar-identified VFR aircraft near fuel dumping may be handled with standard radar traffic advisories.
Apply IFR 1,000-foot-above separation to the VFR aircraft because altitude-only separation always replaces mileage for fuel-dumping operations.
No special separation applies to VFR traffic if the aircraft dumping fuel remains IFR.

items.explanation: Per 9-4-4b, VFR radar-identified aircraft are separated from the aircraft dumping fuel by 5 miles and by the applicable radar procedures.

items.question_text: What minimum separation is provided from a fuel-dumping aircraft to a radar-identified VFR aircraft?

choices.text: 5 miles, with application in accordance with radar procedures.
1,000 feet above only.
2,000 feet below only.
1 mile laterally if the pilot reports the traffic in sight.

items.explanation: Per 9-4-4, VFR radar-identified aircraft are separated from the aircraft dumping fuel by 5 miles and in accordance with the applicable radar procedures.

items.question_text: How often must the advisory be broadcast while fuel dumping is in progress?

choices.text: At 3-minute intervals until the dumping stops.
At 5-minute intervals until the route is clear.
Only once, unless another pilot asks for an update.
Every 10 minutes until the aircraft lands.

items.explanation: Per 9-4-5, facilities concerned broadcast a fuel-dumping advisory at 3-minute intervals until the dumping operation stops, and then a terminating advisory is broadcast.

items.question_text: When may ATC provide vectoring service for a civil operator to jettison external stores at an established drop area?

choices.text: When a written agreement is in effect for that location.
Whenever the pilot agrees that ATC will not be liable.
Only when the operator is using Air Force call signs.
Never; vectoring for jettison is limited to military aircraft.

items.explanation: Per 9-5-1, ATC may vector civil operators and civil aircraft companies for jettisoning external stores only when a written agreement is in effect for that location.

items.question_text: How far in advance of descent is the operator of an unmanned free balloon required to advise ATC?

choices.text: 1 hour in advance of descent.
30 minutes in advance of descent.
Immediately after payload release begins.
Only after the chase plane has radar contact on the balloon.

items.explanation: Per 9-6-1, the operator of an unmanned free balloon is required to advise ATC 1 hour in advance of descent.

items.question_text: If radar contact with a derelict balloon is lost, how often must the advisory be broadcast to affected aircraft?

choices.text: At 10-minute intervals until the derelict balloon is no longer a factor.
At 3-minute intervals until the balloon is reported below Class A.
Only once after radar contact is lost.
At 30-minute intervals until the operator regains communications.

items.explanation: Per 9-6-2, when radar contact with a derelict balloon is lost, an advisory is broadcast every 10 minutes until the balloon is no longer a factor.

items.question_text: When must pertinent information about parachute operations be coordinated with other affected ATC facilities or sectors?

choices.text: Before and at the end of each parachute jump or series of jumps that begins or ends in your area of jurisdiction.
Only after the entire jump operation is complete.
Only when jump operations enter Class B or Class C airspace.
Only if the jump aircraft is IFR.

items.explanation: Per 9-7-1, pertinent information is coordinated prior to and at the end of each jump or series of jumps that begins or ends in your area of jurisdiction.

items.question_text: What is required when authorizing parachute operations in Class A, Class B, or Class C airspace?

choices.text: Authorize them only within airspace designated for the jump activity and separate nonparticipating aircraft from that airspace.
Allow jumpers to drift outside the designated area as long as advisories are issued.
Treat all aircraft in the area as participating traffic until the last jumper lands.
Authorize the operation only after the jump pilot agrees to waive separation from nonparticipating aircraft.

items.explanation: Per 9-7-2, parachute operations in Class A, B, and C airspace are authorized only within the airspace designated for the jump, nonparticipating aircraft are separated from that airspace, and any needed conditions or restrictions may be imposed.

items.question_text: How are requests for parachute jumping in Class D airspace with a functioning control tower handled?

choices.text: Authorize the jump with respect to known or observed traffic and issue advisory information as necessary to both jump and nonparticipating aircraft.
Approve the jump automatically and rely on the pilot to avoid all known traffic.
Deny the jump unless the tower can stop all other traffic in the Class D airspace.
Handle the request the same way as uncontrolled Class E airspace with ATIS-only advisories.

items.explanation: Per 9-7-3, parachute jumping in Class D airspace is authorized with respect to known or observed traffic, and advisory information is issued as necessary for the safe conduct of the jump.

items.question_text: What must advisories to nonparticipating aircraft include for parachute operations in other Class E airspace?

choices.text: The location, time, duration, and altitude from which the jump will be made.
Only the jump aircraft's call sign and destination airport.
Only the drop zone elevation and the number of jumpers aboard.
Only the time the first jumper exits and the expected wind direction on the surface.

items.explanation: Per 9-7-4, advisories to known nonparticipating aircraft must include the location, time, duration, and altitude from which the jump will be made.

items.question_text: What must be done with any reported or observed UAP or unexplained phenomena activity?

choices.text: Inform the operations supervisor or CIC.
Wait until the end of the shift unless the object is identified visually.
Broadcast the report to all aircraft before telling supervisory personnel.
Log it only if it affects separation minima.

items.explanation: Per 9-8-1, any reported or observed UAP or unexplained phenomena activity is reported to the operations supervisor or CIC.
